title: Section 80C Guide — Save Up to ₹46,800 in Tax
meta_description: Section 80C guide 2026 — all investments and expenses that save tax up to ₹1.5 lakh. PPF, ELSS, EPF, LIC, SSY, home loan, tuition — complete comparison.

hero.one_liner: Save up to ₹46,800 in income tax by investing ₹1.5 lakh under Section 80C — complete guide to every eligible investment and the smartest allocation strategy

sections.heading: Overview
content: Section 80C of the Income Tax Act allows you to reduce your taxable income by up to ₹1,50,000 per financial year through specified investments and expenses. This is India's most widely used tax-saving provision — virtually every salaried person and many self-employed individuals use it to save tax and build wealth simultaneously.

The tax saving depends on your tax bracket: 30% bracket (income above ₹10L): Save up to ₹46,800 (₹1.5L × 31.2% including cess). 20% bracket (income ₹5-10L): Save up to ₹31,200. 10% bracket (income ₹2.5-5L): Save up to ₹15,600. 5% bracket (income below ₹2.5L): Save up to ₹7,800.
Critical point: Section 80C deduction is available ONLY under the Old Tax Regime. If you've chosen the New Tax Regime (lower slab rates but no deductions), 80C investments don't save you tax.
Most salaried employees with significant investments/home loan should compare both regimes to see which gives lower total tax. New Regime might be better if you have minimal deductions.

Section 80C has a shared limit of ₹1.5 lakh — meaning all eligible investments/expenses combined cannot exceed ₹1.5 lakh in a financial year. If your EPF contribution already covers ₹1.2 lakh, you have only ₹30,000 of additional 80C room.
This is why planning your 80C allocation early in the year (April-May) prevents panic buying in March when you realize you've underutilized your deduction.

sections.heading: The Smart 80C Strategy — Maximize Returns AND Tax Saving
content:
Step 1: Check what's already covered

Your EPF contribution (12% of basic salary) is automatically deducted from salary and counts towards 80C. Calculate: If basic salary is ₹50,000/month, annual EPF = ₹72,000.
If bonus is ₹1,00,000, EPF on bonus = ₹12,000. Total EPF = ₹84,000.
This leaves ₹66,000 of 80C room for additional investments.

Step 2: Fill remaining with best-return option

For most working professionals under 50 with moderate risk tolerance: invest the remaining amount in ELSS mutual fund via SIP. Why?

ELSS gives: best combination of returns (10-15% historical), short lock-in (3 years), only 80C option with tax-efficient returns (12.5% LTCG above ₹1.25L is tax-free). Start SIP from April 1 itself — don't wait till March.
A ₹5,000/month ELSS SIP from April = ₹60,000 utilized.

Step 3: If risk-averse, use PPF

For people who can't tolerate stock market volatility and sleep badly when markets drop: PPF at 7.1% (completely tax-free) is the next best option. The 15-year lock-in is the trade-off for guaranteed returns and zero risk.
Better than FD because: PPF is tax-free (FD interest is fully taxable), PPF earns 8% vs FD's 7%.

Step 4: Don't forget NPS for EXTRA tax saving

Beyond the ₹1.5L 80C limit, NPS contributions up to ₹50,000 get an ADDITIONAL deduction under Section 80CCD(1B). This saves another ₹15,600 (30% bracket) in tax.
Total tax saving with 80C + NPS: ₹46,800 + ₹15,600 = ₹62,400. If you can afford it, max out both 80C and 80CCD(1B).

Step 5: What NOT to do

Don't buy insurance policies (endowment, ULIP, money-back) just for 80C. Why?
They give only 4-5% returns, lock money for 15-20 years, and charge high commissions. Example: ₹1.5L in endowment at 4.5% for 15 years = ₹2.85L.
Same ₹1.5L in ELSS at 12% = ₹6.3L. That's ₹3.45L difference!
Buy a term insurance plan instead (₹500-1,500/year for ₹1 crore cover, no 80C benefit but better protection) and invest the rest in ELSS/PPF.

sections.heading: 80C Expenses That Save Tax Without Any Investment
content: These are expenses you might already be incurring — claim them under 80C to reduce tax without making new investments:

Tuition fees

Fees paid for full-time education of up to 2 children at any school, college, or university in India. Covers tuition fees only — NOT development fees, hostel, books, transport, or coaching classes.
Both parents can claim for different children. If your child's school fees are ₹2 lakh/year and you have 2 kids, claim ₹4 lakh (but max ₹1.5L total 80C).
Submit: Fee receipts from school/college.

Home loan principal repayment

The principal portion of your home loan EMI qualifies for 80C (interest is separate under Section 24 deduction). Not all of your EMI qualifies — only the principal component.

If you're paying ₹15,000/month EMI with ₹8,000 being principal and ₹7,000 being interest: claim ₹8,000 × 12 = ₹96,000 under 80C. Get an amortization schedule from your lender showing principal/interest split.

Stamp duty and registration charges

The stamp duty and registration charges paid for purchasing a house property qualify for 80C deduction in the year of purchase. This can be substantial (₹50,000 to ₹3+ lakh depending on property value) — claim up to ₹1.5 lakh.
Submit: Copy of registered deed showing stamp duty and registration fees paid.

LIC/insurance premiums

Premiums for life insurance policies (for self, spouse, or children) qualify under 80C. But the premium must not exceed 10% of the sum assured (20% for policies issued before April 1, 2012).

This effectively limits the benefit to term insurance and traditional endowment plans — not ULIPs with high premiums. For a ₹25 lakh term plan at ₹750/year: ✓ Eligible (premium is 0.3% of sum assured).
For a ₹10 lakh ULIP at ₹1.5L/year: ✗ Not eligible (premium is 15% of sum assured).

sections.heading: How to Get Started and Claim

steps.title: Calculate your 80C room in April
steps.description: Total limit: ₹1.5 lakh. Subtract EPF, tuition fees, home loan principal, and insurance premiums ALREADY paid from April. Remaining = room for additional investments.

steps.title: Invest immediately in ELSS or PPF
steps.description: Don't wait till March. Start SIP in ELSS from April 1 itself. If investing ₹60,000, do ₹5,000/month SIP for 12 months. Early investment = more growth due to compounding. By February 28 next year, your investment will have some returns.

steps.title: Collect investment proofs
steps.description: For PPF: Passbook. For ELSS: Fund statements showing investment amount. For home loan: Statement showing principal paid. For tuition: Fee receipts. For insurance: Premium receipt. Keep all originals + photocopies.

steps.title: Submit proofs to employer for TDS reduction
steps.description: Submit investment proofs to your employer's HR/finance team before March 31. They'll adjust your TDS deducted in remaining months or from next month. Less TDS = more take-home salary.

steps.title: Claim in ITR if not submitted to employer
steps.description: While filing your ITR (by July 31), claim all 80C deductions in Schedule EI. Attach copies of proofs. If you didn't submit to employer, excess TDS will be refunded after ITR processing.

faqs.question: Can I claim 80C in the New Tax Regime?
faqs.answer: No. 80C deduction is not available in the New Tax Regime (effective from FY 2023-24). If you've opted for the New Regime, your investments in PPF, ELSS, LIC, EPF, etc. will NOT reduce your taxable income, though you still must contribute EPF if salaried. The New Regime offers lower slab rates instead. For salaried with significant deductions (EPF ₹1L+ + home loan principal ₹5L+), the Old Regime usually saves more tax.

faqs.question: Does EPF automatically count under 80C?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your employee contribution to EPF (12% of basic salary) automatically qualifies under 80C. Employer's contribution does NOT count in your 80C limit (it gets separate treatment — goes to EPS if 10+ years service). If your annual EPF contribution is ₹1.5L or more, your 80C is already maxed out by EPF alone. You cannot claim additional deductions.

faqs.question: Can I claim both 80C and 80CCD(1B) for NPS?
faqs.answer: Yes, strategically. NPS contribution up to ₹1.5L counts under 80C (80CCD(1), within the shared ₹1.5L limit). An ADDITIONAL ₹50,000 NPS contribution gets deduction under Section 80CCD(1B) — this is OVER AND ABOVE the ₹1.5L 80C limit. So total NPS tax benefit: ₹2L deduction (₹1.5L under 80C + ₹50K under 80CCD(1B)). Strategy: Invest ₹1.5L under 80C in highest-return option (ELSS), then invest ₹50K in NPS for extra ₹50K deduction.

faqs.question: I invested ₹2 lakh in PPF this year. Can I claim all of it under 80C?
faqs.answer: No. PPF has an annual deposit limit of ₹1.5 lakh/year, so you shouldn't have been able to deposit ₹2L legally. If you somehow did, the excess is returned by PPF. For 80C deduction, maximum claim is ₹1.5L across ALL investments combined in a financial year.

faqs.question: Which 80C investment gives the best returns after tax?
faqs.answer: Ranking (after-tax returns for a 30% bracket person): (1) PPF at 7.1% tax-free (effectively 10.1% pre-tax equivalent), (2) ELSS at 12-15% with LTCG benefit (effective ~10.5% after tax), (3) EPF at 8.25% tax-free (effective 11.8% pre-tax equivalent). FD at 6.5% becomes only 4.5% after 30% tax — worst option. VPF (Voluntary PF) is actually better than both — 8.25% tax-free with withdrawal flexibility.

disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only. Consult a qualified financial advisor before making investment decisions.
